chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing pediatric dyslipidemia focus on LDL-C and non-HDL-C thresholds, lifestyle interventions, and statin safety monitoring, especially in high-risk cases like familial hypercholesterolemia or youth-onset T2DM.
learning_objectives: Differentiate between insulin-resistance lipid patterns and LDL-C-driven patterns in children.
Apply pediatric LDL-C and non-HDL-C cutpoints to guide dyslipidemia management decisions.
Understand statin initiation criteria and safety monitoring protocols in pediatric patients.
Recognize familial hypercholesterolemia and initiate timely interventions.
key_facts: Acceptable LDL-C is <110 mg/dL; borderline-high is 110–129 mg/dL; high is ≥130 mg/dL.
Non-HDL-C thresholds: acceptable <120 mg/dL, borderline-high 120–144 mg/dL, high ≥145 mg/dL.
Familial hypercholesterolemia is strongly suspected with LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL or ≥160 mg/dL with family history.
Statins are indicated when LDL-C targets are unmet in high-risk cases like youth-onset T2DM or familial hypercholesterolemia.
Baseline liver enzymes and muscle toxicity risk must be assessed before starting statins, with follow-up labs 4–12 weeks after initiation or dose changes.
reference_quote: Statins are used when LDL-C targets are not met despite lifestyle therapy in high-risk contexts such as youth-onset T2DM or probable familial hypercholesterolemia. (NHLBI, 2011)
document_key_topics: Statin safety and monitoring in children
